scenario.source_text: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. Yesterday in ED majors I assessed a 62-year-old man with pleuritic chest pain. I considered ACS, PE and pneumonia, used Wells score, reviewed ECG and CXR, discussed with my consultant, and arranged CTPA. I learned to state my risk assessment more clearly when escalating.

recommended_forms.rationale: The trainee managed a 62-year-old with pleuritic chest pain, formulated a differential diagnosis, used a clinical decision tool, interpreted investigations, and discussed the case retrospectively with a consultant.
The trainee includes a specific reflection on their communication style, identifying a lesson learned about clearly stating their risk assessment when escalating clinical cases.

draft_fields.clinical_reasoning:
I assessed a 62-year-old man presenting with pleuritic chest pain. My differential diagnosis included acute coronary syndrome (ACS), pulmonary embolism (PE), and pneumonia.

To risk-stratify the patient, I calculated a Wells score, reviewed the electrocardiogram (ECG), and examined the chest X-ray (CXR). I discussed my findings and clinical reasoning with the ED consultant, and we agreed to arrange a CT pulmonary angiogram (CTPA) to definitively rule out a PE.
draft_fields.reflection:
This case of structured communication during clinical escalation. I learned that I need to state my formal risk assessments, such as the Wells score, more clearly and explicitly when discussing cases with senior colleagues to a shared understanding of clinical risk.

In future, I will lead my senior discussions with a clear summary of the objective risk scores alongside my clinical impression to streamline the escalation and decision-making process.
key_capabilities: SLO1 KC1: to be expert in assessing and managing all adult patients attending the ED. These capabilities will apply to patients attending with both physical and psychological ill health (2025 Update)

scenario.source_text: Voice transcript: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. Today I performed DC cardioversion for unstable atrial fibrillation as a formal DOPS. My consultant observed each step directly. I checked consent, monitoring and sedation plan, delivered a synchronised shock, and reflected on calling for airway support earlier.

recommended_forms.rationale: Directly observed hands-on procedure with senior supervision; DOPS is the specific assessed-procedure form.
The performed procedure can also be recorded in the procedural log.
Feedback/reflection was included alongside the procedural assessment.

draft_fields.trainee_performance: I performed a direct observation of procedural skills (DOPS) for DC cardioversion under the direct supervision of my consultant. I checked patient consent, ensured appropriate monitoring was in place, and prepared the sedation plan. I then successfully delivered a synchronised shock.
draft_fields.reflection: On reflection, I should have called for airway support earlier in the preparation phase. While the sedation and procedure went smoothly, airway support is on standby earlier provides a safer margin for the patient. In future cases, I will make this call as part of my initial preparation checklist.
key_capabilities: SLO6 KC2: the knowledge and psychomotor skills to perform EM procedural skills safely and in a timely fashion (2025 Update)
SLO3 KC2: be expert in fluid management and circulatory support in critically ill patients (2025 Update)
SLO3 KC3: manage all the life-threatening conditions including peri-arrest & arrest situations in the ED (2025 Update)

scenario.form_name: Direct Observation of Procedural Skills - ACCS
scenario.source_text: Document extract from a portfolio note: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. During my ACCS anaesthetic placement I inserted a chest drain using Seldinger technique as an observed DOPS on 5 July 2026. My supervisor watched the procedure. I confirmed landmarks, used sterile technique and reflected on slowing down before dilating.

recommended_forms.rationale: ACCS procedural skill with direct observation; use the ACCS DOPS form.
The same ACCS procedure can also be recorded in the ACCS procedural log.

draft_fields.case_observed: I inserted a chest drain using the Seldinger technique under direct supervision. I confirmed the anatomical landmarks, maintained strict sterile technique, and performed the procedure safely.
draft_fields.reflection:
This procedure reminded me of the importance of slowing down and each step is perfectly prepared before moving on to the next, specifically before dilating over the guidewire. 

I will continue to focus on deliberate practice and maintaining a calm, systematic approach during invasive procedures.
key_capabilities: SLO6 KC2: the knowledge and psychomotor skills to perform EM procedural skills safely and in a timely fashion (2025 Update)
SLO9 KC1: be able to undertake training and supervision of members of the ED team in the clinical environment (2025 Update)

scenario.source_text: Context supplied with image: the image is supporting evidence only; do not infer findings beyond this text. I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. The activity happened yesterday. A consultant directly observed me taking a focused history and examination from a 7-year-old with wheeze in paediatric ED. I explained the plan to the parent and received feedback on structuring my assessment.

recommended_forms.rationale: The trainee was directly observed by a consultant while performing a focused history, clinical examination, and communicating the management plan to the parent in real time.

draft_fields.clinical_reasoning: I took a focused history and performed a clinical examination on a 7-year-old child presenting to the paediatric ED with a wheeze.
draft_fields.reflection: The consultant directly observed the encounter and provided feedback on structuring my assessment. I will use this feedback to refine my approach to paediatric history taking and examination in future presentations.
key_capabilities: SLO5 KC1: be expert in assessing and managing all children and young adult patients attending the ED (2025 Update)

SLO2 KC1: able to support the pre-hospital, medical, nursing and administrative team in answering clinical questions and in making safe decisions for patients with appropriate levels of risk in the ED (2025 Update)
SLO9 KC1: be able to undertake training and supervision of members of the ED team in the clinical environment (2025 Update)

scenario.source_text: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. The activity happened yesterday. On an evening acute take I managed six patients across majors and resus, including sepsis, chest pain and a frail fall. My consultant observed my prioritisation, handover and escalation decisions over the shift.

recommended_forms.rationale: The trainee managed a cohort of six patients across majors and resuscitation over an acute take shift, with a consultant observing their prioritisation, clinical decision-making, and escalation.

draft_fields.cases_observed: During an evening acute take, I managed six patients across majors and resuscitation. The cases included presentations of sepsis, chest pain, and a frail patient who had suffered a fall. My consultant observed my prioritisation, handover, and escalation decisions throughout the shift.
draft_fields.reflection:
Managing multiple patients across different areas of the department required continuous prioritisation to clinical safety. I had to balance the immediate needs of a patient in resuscitation with the ongoing management and investigation of patients in majors. 

Discussing my escalation and handover decisions with the consultant helped me refine how I structure communication during busy shifts. I need to that handovers remain concise and focused on outstanding key investigations and clear contingency plans. 

I will continue to focus on maintaining situational awareness across different zones of the department, that sick patients are escalated early and that flow is maintained safely.
key_capabilities: SLO2 KC1: able to support the pre-hospital, medical, nursing and administrative team in answering clinical questions and in making safe decisions for patients with appropriate levels of risk in the ED (2025 Update)
SLO8 KC3: will maintain situational awareness throughout the shift to ensure safety is optimised (2025 Update)
SLO8 KC4: will anticipate challenges, generate options, make decisions and communicate these effectively to the team as lead clinician (2025 Update)

scenario.source_text: Voice transcript: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. The activity happened yesterday. I acted as EPIC for a crowded evening ED shift. I allocated junior doctors, held a safety huddle, escalated capacity concerns to site management and reflected on protecting time for re-review of high-risk patients.

recommended_forms.rationale: The trainee acted in the EPIC (Emergency Physician in Charge) leadership role during a crowded shift, coordinating junior doctors, leading a safety huddle, and managing departmental escalation.

The trainee actively reflects on the shift-level experience, specifically identifying a learning point regarding the cognitive and operational challenge of protecting time to re-review high-risk patients.

draft_fields.leadership_context: I was acting as the Doctor in Charge (EPIC) during a crowded evening shift in the Emergency Department, managing junior doctor allocations and department capacity under significant pressure.
draft_fields.clinical_reasoning:
I took on the role of EPIC during a busy and crowded evening shift. My management focused on operational flow and patient safety across the department.

I dynamically allocated junior doctors to clinical areas to match patient demand and acuity. To team alignment and situational awareness, I held a safety huddle with the nursing and medical staff, identifying high-risk patients and potential bottlenecks. 

As crowding increased, I escalated capacity concerns to the hospital site management team to coordinate wider trust support and patient flow out of the department.
draft_fields.reflection:
Managing a crowded department as EPIC requires balancing macro-level flow with individual patient safety. I realised the importance of maintaining oversight rather than getting drawn into individual clinical tasks.

I reflected on the need to actively protect my own time to allow for the re-review of high-risk patients. When the department is congested, these patients are at the greatest risk of delay or clinical deterioration.

I will continue to refine my threshold for early escalation to site management. I will that safeguarding time for structured reviews of vulnerable patients remains a priority during high-occupancy shifts.
key_capabilities: SLO8 KC1: will provide support to ED staff at all levels and disciplines on the ED shift (2025 Update)
SLO8 KC2: will be able to liaise with the rest of the acute/urgent care team and wider hospital as shift leader (2025 Update)
SLO8 KC3: will maintain situational awareness throughout the shift to ensure safety is optimised (2025 Update)
SLO8 KC4: will anticipate challenges, generate options, make decisions and communicate these effectively to the team as lead clinician (2025 Update)

scenario.source_text: Document extract from a portfolio note: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. The activity happened yesterday. I appraised evidence on using age-adjusted D-dimer for suspected PE. I framed a PICO question, searched NICE and a recent meta-analysis, and discussed how I would apply the evidence safely in low-risk patients. I learned to document the limits of the evidence when explaining risk to patients.

recommended_forms.rationale: The trainee formulated a structured clinical question (PICO), searched literature (NICE guidelines and a meta-analysis), and appraised evidence regarding age-adjusted D-dimer for PE to apply it to clinical practice.
The trainee reflects on their learning, specifically noting how they will change their future practice regarding documenting the limitations of evidence when discussing risk with patients.

draft_fields.situation: I appraised evidence on using age-adjusted D-dimer for suspected PE.
draft_fields.search_methodology: I searched NICE and a recent meta-analysis.
draft_fields.apply_to_practice: I discussed how I would apply the evidence safely in low-risk patients.
draft_fields.communicate_to_patient: I learned to document the limits of the evidence when explaining risk to patients.
draft_fields.reflection: I learned to document the limits of the evidence when explaining risk to patients.
key_capabilities: SLO10 KC1: be able to appraise, synthesise, communicate and use research evidence to develop EM care (2025 Update)

SLO2 KC1: able to support the pre-hospital, medical, nursing and administrative team in answering clinical questions and in making safe decisions for patients with appropriate levels of risk in the ED (2025 Update)

A consultant observed the session and gave feedback on pacing and checking understanding.

recommended_forms.rationale: The trainee delivered a formal, observed 25-minute teaching session to junior doctors, and a consultant assessor was present to observe and evaluate the teaching.
The consultant observed the teaching session specifically to provide structured feedback on teaching skills, such as pacing and checking for learner understanding.

scenario.source_text: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. The activity happened yesterday. I reviewed feedback from nurses, juniors and consultants. Themes were calm leadership and clear explanations, with a development point around documenting senior discussions more consistently.

recommended_forms.rationale: The trainee has gathered and reviewed feedback from multiple colleague groups (nurses, juniors, consultants) regarding their clinical performance and leadership.
The trainee is reflecting on multi-source feedback themes (calm leadership, documentation of senior discussions) to guide their professional development.

draft_fields.context: I reviewed the compiled feedback from my Multi-Source Feedback (MSF), which included responses from nursing staff, junior doctors, and consultants during my ST5 placement.
draft_fields.reflection:
The feedback was reassuring, particularly the positive themes around my calm leadership style and ability to provide clear explanations to the team during busy shifts.

An important development point raised was the need to document senior discussions more consistently in the patient notes. I will focus on all consultant inputs and management decisions are clearly recorded in real time.

scenario.source_text: Voice transcript: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. The activity happened yesterday. I led a QI project on time to antibiotics in adult sepsis. Baseline audit was 42 cases, we introduced a triage prompt and re-audit showed improvement from 58 to 39 minutes. I presented it at governance. My PDP summary this year is to improve quality improvement leadership. Next year I will focus on embedding the prompt and supporting re-audit.

recommended_forms.rationale: QI/audit project with measurement and change; QIAT is the specific assessment form.

draft_fields.qi_engagement: I led a QI project on time to antibiotics in adult sepsis. I presented the project at our departmental governance meeting.
draft_fields.qi_understanding: This developed my practical understanding of using baseline measurement, an intervention and re-audit to assess change in an ED quality improvement project.
draft_fields.reflection:
I led a quality improvement project addressing time to antibiotics in adult sepsis within our department.

We analyzed a baseline audit of 42 cases to understand our initial performance. To address delays, we introduced a specific sepsis prompt at triage. Our re-audit demonstrated an improvement in the average time to antibiotics, reducing it from 58 to 39 minutes.

Leading this project helped me understand the practical challenges of implementing triage-level interventions. Presenting these findings at the governance meeting allowed me to engage the wider team, which is vital for sustaining clinical change.
draft_fields.next_pdp: Next year I will focus on embedding the triage prompt further and supporting the next cycle of re-audit to the improvements are sustained.
key_capabilities: SLO11 KC1: be able to provide clinical leadership on effective Quality Improvement work (2025 Update)
SLO11 KC2: be able to support and develop a culture of departmental safety, and good clinical governance (2025 Update)
SLO12 KC2: be able to investigate a patient safety incident, participate and contribute effectively to department clinical governance activities and risk reduction processes (2025 Update)

scenario.source_text: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. The activity happened yesterday. I performed a shoulder reduction in ED with senior advice available but no formal DOPS. I documented neurovascular status before and after and reflected on analgesia planning.

recommended_forms.rationale: The trainee personally performed a shoulder reduction procedure, but no formal assessor was present to observe and complete a DOPS checklist.
The trainee reflects on their decision-making process, specifically the clinical planning around pre- and post-reduction analgesia and neurovascular documentation.

draft_fields.reflective_comments:
I performed a shoulder reduction in the emergency department with senior advice available. I that I documented the patient's neurovascular status both before and after the procedure to monitor for any nerve or vascular compromise.

I reflected on my analgesia planning for this procedure, considering how to optimise patient comfort and muscle relaxation to a smoother reduction. In future, I will continue to pre- and post-procedural neurovascular documentation and early, effective analgesia planning.

scenario.source_text: Voice transcript: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. The activity happened yesterday. On ACCS I performed a lumbar puncture under supervision. I checked contraindications, used sterile technique, obtained CSF samples and reflected on patient positioning.

recommended_forms.rationale: ACCS procedural skill without a clear direct-assessment signal; use the ACCS procedural log.

draft_fields.brief_description: I performed a lumbar puncture under supervision. I checked for contraindications before the procedure, maintained a strict sterile technique throughout, and successfully obtained CSF samples for analysis.
draft_fields.reflective_comments: I reflected on the importance of patient positioning during this procedure. The patient is fully flexed and properly aligned makes identifying the landmarks easier and increases the likelihood of a successful first pass.
key_capabilities: SLO6 KC2: the knowledge and psychomotor skills to perform EM procedural skills safely and in a timely fashion (2025 Update)

scenario.source_text: Document extract from a portfolio note: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. The activity happened yesterday. I completed a self-directed learning module on paediatric fever pathways, then applied the learning by reviewing our local traffic-light guidance and changing my safety-netting.

recommended_forms.rationale: The trainee completed an independent self-directed learning module on paediatric fever pathways to update their clinical knowledge.
The trainee reflects on how they applied this new knowledge to local traffic-light guidelines and modified their personal safety-netting practice.

draft_fields.reflection_title: Self-directed learning: Paediatric fever pathways and safety-netting
draft_fields.resource_details: RCEMlearning module on paediatric fever pathways, alongside a review of local traffic-light guidance.
draft_fields.reflection:
I completed a self-directed learning module on paediatric fever pathways. I then applied this learning by reviewing our local traffic-light guidance.

Following this review, I have adapted my clinical practice by changing my safety-netting approach for paediatric patients presenting with fever. This that parents and carers are provided with specific, structured advice aligned with our local and national pathways.

scenario.source_text: Context supplied with image: the image is supporting evidence only; do not infer findings beyond this text. I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. The activity happened yesterday. I performed lung ultrasound on a breathless patient with suspected pulmonary oedema. I identified bilateral B-lines, discussed images with my consultant and reflected on integrating ultrasound with clinical assessment.

recommended_forms.rationale: Visible source documents a procedure; log the procedure unless a formal observed assessment is confirmed.
Use if an assessor directly observed the documented procedure.
Directly matches the performance and reflection of a point-of-care lung ultrasound to assess for pulmonary oedema.

draft_fields.clinical_scenario: I assessed a breathless patient with suspected pulmonary oedema.
draft_fields.how_used: I performed a lung ultrasound to evaluate for signs of pulmonary congestion and pleural pathology.
draft_fields.usable_images: I was able to obtain usable images, identifying bilateral B-lines.
draft_fields.interpret_images: I interpreted the presence of bilateral B-lines as supportive of pulmonary oedema and discussed these findings with my consultant.
draft_fields.learning_points: I reflected on how to integrate point-of-care ultrasound findings directly with my clinical assessment to narrow down the differential diagnosis in acute breathlessness.

scenario.source_text: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. The activity happened yesterday. I reflected on a complaint about delayed communication with a family during a busy resus shift. The clinical care was appropriate, but I should have allocated a clearer update role and documented conversations better.

recommended_forms.rationale: The trainee is directly reflecting on a patient/family complaint about delayed communication, focusing on what happened, the learning points regarding communication roles, and future documentation practice.

The entry contains strong reflective elements ('I reflected on', 'I should have allocated', 'I realize') regarding non-clinical aspects of patient care, making a general reflective log highly appropriate.

As an ST5 trainee, if this reflection is used to satisfy the Management Portfolio requirement by reviewing the complaint from a departmental systems perspective (e.g., analyzing communication delays in Resus), this specific management form is appropriate.

draft_fields.reflection_title: Reflection on complaint regarding communication during a busy resuscitation shift
draft_fields.key_features: I received a complaint regarding delayed communication with a patient's family during a highly active resuscitation shift. While the clinical care delivered was appropriate, there was a breakdown in keeping the family updated.
draft_fields.key_aspects: I managed a patient in the resuscitation room during a busy shift. Although the clinical management itself was appropriate, I did not assign a specific team member to update the family, nor did I thoroughly document the communication that did occur.
draft_fields.learning_points:
I learned that during high-pressure resuscitation scenarios, communication with relatives must be managed as a distinct task. Even when clinical care is optimal, a lack of structured updates causes distress to families. 

I need to that a designated staff member is explicitly allocated the role of family liaison during major resuscitations, rather than assuming updates will happen organically.
draft_fields.further_action: I will explicitly assign a team member to update relatives during future resuscitations. I will also that all communication, including unsuccessful attempts to contact family or brief verbal updates, is documented clearly in the medical records.
key_capabilities: SLO7 KC1: have expert communication skills to negotiate, manage complicated or evolving interactions (2025 Update)
SLO12 KC6: demonstrate a positive impact on the culture of the Emergency Department through attitudes and behaviours that impact positively on colleagues, patients and their relatives (2025 Update)
SLO3 KC3: manage all the life-threatening conditions including peri-arrest & arrest situations in the ED (2025 Update)

scenario.source_text: Voice transcript: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. The activity happened yesterday. I contributed to a serious incident review after delayed recognition of sepsis in majors. I reviewed the timeline, identified handover gaps and learned to use an explicit trigger for senior review.

recommended_forms.rationale: The trainee actively contributed to a formal serious incident review regarding delayed sepsis recognition and is reflecting on the clinical and system-level learning.
The trainee is reflecting on a significant clinical event, identifying personal learning points around handover gaps and thresholds for senior review.

draft_fields.reflection_title: Serious Incident Review: Delayed Recognition of Sepsis
draft_fields.description: I contributed to a serious incident review following the delayed recognition of sepsis in a patient managed in the majors area of the Emergency Department.
draft_fields.root_causes: The review of the timeline identified critical gaps during patient handovers, which led to a delay in recognizing the evolving severity of the patient's sepsis.
draft_fields.contributing_factors: Key contributing factors included unstructured handover communication and the lack of a clear, standardized trigger to prompt a senior clinical review when a patient's physiological parameters deteriorated.
draft_fields.learning_points: I learned the critical importance of handover processes in majors. I recognized that relying on unstructured updates is insufficient and that we must utilize explicit, objective physiological triggers to mandate an immediate senior review.
draft_fields.further_action: I will apply these findings by strictly utilizing structured handover formats during my shifts. I will advocate for and implement explicit senior review triggers for patients showing signs of worsening sepsis in majors.
key_capabilities: SLO11 KC2: be able to support and develop a culture of departmental safety, and good clinical governance (2025 Update)
SLO12 KC2: be able to investigate a patient safety incident, participate and contribute effectively to department clinical governance activities and risk reduction processes (2025 Update)

SLO2 KC1: able to support the pre-hospital, medical, nursing and administrative team in answering clinical questions and in making safe decisions for patients with appropriate levels of risk in the ED (2025 Update)

I attended a regional paediatric emergency medicine teaching day covering safeguarding, sepsis and non-accidental injury. I reflected on using the learning in future paediatric assessments.

draft_fields.title_of_education: Regional paediatric emergency medicine teaching day
draft_fields.learning_points:
I attended this regional training session which covered key areas of paediatric emergency care, specifically focusing on safeguarding, sepsis, and non-accidental injury (NAI).

I reflected on how to integrate these updates into my clinical practice, I maintain a high index of suspicion for safeguarding and NAI during my paediatric assessments, and remain structured in my approach to identifying and managing paediatric sepsis.

I reflected on how a systematic approach that life-threatening injuries are not missed in the high-pressure resuscitation room environment.

scenario.source_text: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. The activity happened yesterday. My consultant assessed me during a three-hour evening ED shift. I managed flow in majors, supported juniors, reviewed two unwell patients and reflected on maintaining situational awareness under pressure.

recommended_forms.rationale: Direct consultant observation of shift-level performance (3 hours) focusing on non-technical skills including flow management, supporting juniors, and situational awareness.
Allows for structured personal reflection on the trainee's performance, specifically regarding the maintenance of situational awareness and clinical decision-making under high-pressure conditions.

draft_fields.reflection:
During this three-hour evening shift, I acted as the shift leader under consultant observation. I managed flow in the majors area, supported junior colleagues with clinical decisions, and directly reviewed two unwell patients. Balancing direct clinical reviews with the supervisory and administrative oversight required to maintain departmental flow was a challenge.

I realised the importance of maintaining strong situational awareness under pressure. When reviewing unwell patients, it is easy to lose oversight of the wider department. I worked to consciously step back, review the ambulance arrival screen, and check in with the nursing charge nurse to our resources were distributed safely.

In future shifts, I will continue to practice this balance. I plan to establish regular, brief touchpoints with the nurse in charge and the junior doctors to maintain a high-level view of departmental safety without getting prematurely anchored in individual complex cases.
key_capabilities: SLO8 KC1: will provide support to ED staff at all levels and disciplines on the ED shift (2025 Update)
SLO8 KC3: will maintain situational awareness throughout the shift to ensure safety is optimised (2025 Update)
SLO8 KC4: will anticipate challenges, generate options, make decisions and communicate these effectively to the team as lead clinician (2025 Update)

scenario.source_text: Voice transcript: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. The activity happened yesterday. I managed a breathless patient where I initially anchored on COPD. Senior review prompted a PE workup. I learned to pause when the story does not fit and to document my differential more explicitly.

recommended_forms.rationale: The trainee managed a patient with acute breathlessness, requiring clinical reasoning, differential diagnosis formulation (COPD vs. PE), and retrospective discussion with a senior clinician.
The trainee explicitly reflects on cognitive bias (anchoring on COPD), the value of senior review, and specific changes to their future practice (pausing and explicitly documenting differentials).

draft_fields.reflection_title: Reflecting on anchoring bias in a breathless patient
draft_fields.reflection:
I managed a breathless patient where I initially anchored on COPD. I focused on this respiratory history during my initial assessment and started standard treatment.

During a senior review, the consultant prompted me to consider a pulmonary embolism (PE) workup, as some aspects of the clinical picture did not fully align with a simple COPD flare. This prompted us to expand the investigations and adjust the management plan.
draft_fields.replay_differently: I would initiate a deliberate diagnostic pause to actively challenge my working diagnosis and broader differential before finalizing the treatment plan.
draft_fields.why: I anchored too quickly on the patient's known history of COPD, which created a cognitive shortcut and led me to overlook features that pointed towards a PE.
draft_fields.different_outcome: The clinical outcome was positive due to the senior intervention, but escalating with a more structured differential earlier would have expedited the correct diagnostic pathway and reduced the delay in starting targeted therapy.
draft_fields.focussing_on: I am focussing on implementing a cognitive forcing strategy during my initial assessments. Specifically, I will use a deliberate re-evaluation pause to explicitly document and challenge my top three differential diagnoses, particularly when treating patients with chronic respiratory conditions who present with acute breathlessness.
draft_fields.learned: I have learned that having a known chronic disease like COPD can easily lead to anchoring bias. I must actively look for mismatching clinical features that warrant a wider workup, such as for a PE.
key_capabilities: SLO1 KC1: to be expert in assessing and managing all adult patients attending the ED. These capabilities will apply to patients attending with both physical and psychological ill health (2025 Update)

SLO2 KC1: able to support the pre-hospital, medical, nursing and administrative team in answering clinical questions and in making safe decisions for patients with appropriate levels of risk in the ED (2025 Update)

scenario.source_text: Document extract from a portfolio note: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. The activity happened yesterday. A consultant observed me teaching an F2 doctor how to assess ankle injuries. They fed back that my explanations were clear but I should check the learner's baseline knowledge first.

recommended_forms.rationale: The user explicitly asked for Teaching Delivered By Trainee.

draft_fields.reflection:
An Emergency Medicine consultant observed me teaching an F2 doctor how to assess ankle injuries in the clinical environment. 

The consultant fed back that my explanations were clear and the teaching was well-structured. However, they noted that I should check the learner's baseline knowledge first before delivering the content, rather than assuming their current level of understanding.

In future teaching sessions, I will start with a brief, informal Q&A or discussion to gauge the learner's baseline knowledge. This will allow me to tailor my teaching more effectively to their specific learning needs and avoid repeating information they are already comfortable with.

scenario.source_text: Document extract from a portfolio note: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. The activity happened yesterday. I completed an audit of documentation of capacity assessments in ED mental health presentations. I reviewed 30 notes, presented results and proposed a documentation prompt.

recommended_forms.rationale: The user explicitly asked for Audit Assessment Tool.

draft_fields.reflection:
I completed an audit of the documentation of capacity assessments in ED mental health presentations, reviewing 30 sets of notes. This review highlighted variability in how clinical capacity is recorded during acute psychiatric presentations in the emergency department.

To address the gaps identified in the documentation, I presented the results to the department and proposed a structured documentation prompt. Implementing this prompt aims to standardise recording practices, medicolegal documentation and improving patient safety.

Through this project, I learned how to systematically evaluate clinical records to identify areas for safety improvement. I will monitor the uptake of the documentation prompt and plan to re-audit the notes to complete the cycle and assess the impact of this intervention.

scenario.source_text: Context supplied with image: the image is supporting evidence only; do not infer findings beyond this text. I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. The activity happened yesterday. I recruited patients to an ED research study after completing GCP training. I checked eligibility, explained the study, obtained consent and reflected on protecting clinical flow.

recommended_forms.rationale: This captures the trainee's active role in recruiting patients for a clinical trial, managing the consent process, and balancing research participation with active department flow.

Allows the trainee to reflect on the practicalities of executing GCP training in a busy ED, managing clinical flow while recruiting, and the ethical considerations of patient consent in acute settings.
Documents the formal Good Clinical Practice (GCP) research training completed by the trainee as a learner, which enabled them to participate in the study recruitment.

draft_fields.reflection: Yesterday, I recruited patients to an ED research study. I was responsible for checking patient eligibility against the study criteria, explaining the trial details clearly to potential participants, and obtaining formal informed consent. This required me to balance research participation with maintaining clinical flow in a busy emergency department. I had to that taking the time to discuss the study and complete the paperwork did not compromise active patient care or departmental throughput. In future, I will continue to identify windows where research recruitment can be integrated smoothly without disrupting acute clinical duties.
key_capabilities: SLO10 KC2: be able to actively participate in research (2025 Update)
